---
title: "blobsim: an energy-conserving world of blobs"
jupyter: brainvomit
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

> _Scaffold — prose and interpretation to be filled in. Headings follow the order
> of discovery; the charts and animations are seeded from a real run._

## What it is

`blobsim` is a small world that runs on a grid. Each cell can hold one **blob** — a
simple creature with an energy level. Step by step, every blob senses its
neighbours, picks an action (move, eat, reproduce, or rest), and pays for it in
energy. Blobs that run out of energy die; well-fed ones reproduce. The grid itself
holds energy that blobs eat and that may or may not replenish over time.

The one rule the whole world obeys is **conservation of energy**: energy is never
created or destroyed, only moved between blobs, the grid, and a "spent" pile. That
single constraint is what makes the world's behaviour predictable — and checkable.

<!-- TODO: what question this world is meant to explore. -->

## How it is designed

The grid wraps around at the edges (a blob walking off the right side reappears on
the left). Every step runs the same fixed sequence:

> observe → decide → resolve who-goes-where → charge energy → act → absorb from the
> grid → update the environment → remove the dead → record

A few deliberate choices:

- **Energy ledger.** A running tally checks, every single step, that all the energy
  still adds up — so a bug that quietly leaks energy can't hide.
- **Repeatable.** The same starting seed always produces the exact same run.
- **Swappable pieces.** A blob's behaviour ("rules"), the environment's behaviour
  (decaying vs. replenishing), and how movement conflicts are settled can each be
  swapped out without touching the rest.

<!-- TODO: more on the rules/sensing model and the action costs, in your words. -->

## Inspiration

It starts from Conway's *Game of Life* — cells on a grid that live or die by their
neighbours — and asks what happens when each cell becomes an individual that has to
*earn its living*: carry energy, spend it to act, and starve if it can't. The result
sits somewhere between a cellular automaton and a tiny ecology.

<!-- TODO: the personal hook — why this was worth building. -->

```{python}
#| label: setup
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "analysis")   # dev-only data (gitignored)
import json
import pandas as pd
import plotly.io as pio
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
pio.templates.default = "plotly"

import blobsim
```

## The world in motion

A finite grid with no replenishment can only end one way — the blobs eat through
the available energy and die out:

![Two kinds of blob draw down a finite grid until none are left.](images/blobsim/sc1_decay_extinction.gif)

Here is one run's population and total energy over time:

```{python}
#| label: live-sim
from blobsim import (
    Simulation, SimulationConfig, SpeciesConfig, BlobAttributes,
    RegeneratingEnvironment, ActionType, SensingLevel, MOORE,
)
from blobsim.species import GrazerRules

attrs = BlobAttributes.create(
    species="grazer", max_energy=10.0, offspring_energy=3.0,
    reproduction_threshold=7.0, base_metabolic_cost=0.1, move_cost=0.2,
    reproduce_cost=0.5, observation_radius=1, sensing_level=SensingLevel.BASIC,
    allowed_actions=frozenset(ActionType), allowed_directions=MOORE,
)
config = SimulationConfig(
    grid_size=30,
    environment=RegeneratingEnvironment(rate=0.05, capacity=2.0, initial_cell_energy=1.0),
    species=[SpeciesConfig(rules_factory=GrazerRules, attributes=attrs, count=25, initial_energy=5.0)],
    seed=42,
)
sim = Simulation(config)
traj = pd.DataFrame([
    {"step": s.step, "population": s.n_alive, "total_energy": s.e_blobs + s.e_grid}
    for s in sim.iterate(steps=200)
])

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_scatter(x=traj.step, y=traj.population, name="population", yaxis="y1")
fig.add_scatter(x=traj.step, y=traj.total_energy, name="total energy (blobs + grid)", yaxis="y2")
fig.update_layout(
    height=380, margin=dict(l=0, r=0, t=10, b=0), xaxis_title="step",
    yaxis=dict(title="population"),
    yaxis2=dict(title="energy", overlaying="y", side="right", showgrid=False),
    legend=dict(orientation="h", y=1.12),
)
fig
```

<!-- TODO: read off the population dynamics. -->

## Checking the world against the math

Every behaviour below is first predicted *from the rules alone* — a conservation
law, an expected lifespan, a stable population — and the simulation is judged
against that prediction. The full set of checks from the last run:

```{python}
#| label: bench-table
LABELS = {
    "B2a": "Single-blob mean lifespan",
    "SC-1": "Guaranteed extinction on a finite grid",
    "SC-4": "Boom then bust",
    "SC-5": "Carrying capacity",
    "SC-6": "Energy steady-state",
}
res = json.load(open("analysis/blobsim/benchmark_results.json"))
df = pd.DataFrame(res["results"])
df["check"] = df["id"].map(LABELS).fillna(df["id"])
df[["check", "predicted", "observed_mean", "tolerance", "passed"]]
```

```{python}
#| label: bench-chart
num = df.dropna(subset=["predicted", "observed_mean"])
fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_bar(x=num.check, y=num.predicted, name="predicted")
fig.add_bar(x=num.check, y=num.observed_mean, name="observed")
fig.update_layout(barmode="group", height=340, margin=dict(l=0, r=0, t=10, b=0),
                  legend=dict(orientation="h", y=1.15), yaxis_title="value")
fig
```

<!-- TODO: comment on how closely observation tracks prediction. -->

## What the runs show

### A single blob's lifespan

Left alone on a draining grid, a blob lives a predictable number of steps on
average — close to what the energy math says it should.

<!-- TODO: the two-phase intuition (active while it can afford to move, then resting
until it starves). -->

### Boom, then bust

Give blobs a rich grid and they multiply fast — until the crowd outstrips the food
and the population collapses.

![A population explodes to fill the grid, then crashes.](images/blobsim/sc4_boom_bust.gif)

<!-- TODO: why the peak is set by the size of the grid, not the total energy. -->

### Settling at a carrying capacity

With energy replenishing, the population stops growing at a stable level — and it
reaches roughly the same level whether it starts crowded or sparse.

![Starting crowded, the population relaxes toward a steady level.](images/blobsim/sc5_dense.gif)

<!-- TODO: the surprise — the steady level lands ~25% above the simple prediction,
because real blobs spread out and waste fewer moves on collisions than the math
assumes. -->

### A lone blob's energy

A single blob on a rich, replenishing grid quickly fills up to its maximum and
stays pinned there.

<!-- TODO. -->

## Competition between kinds

Put two kinds of blob on the same grid and they compete for the same energy:

![Two kinds of blob compete for one grid.](images/blobsim/c3_competition.gif)

<!-- TODO: who wins, and what decides it. -->

## What I took away, in order

<!-- TODO: ordered takeaways, e.g.
1. Conservation is the backbone — every check is ultimately energy bookkeeping.
2. On a finite grid, extinction is guaranteed; reproduction only moves energy around.
3. Crowding, not total energy, caps the population at its peak.
4. The simplest population prediction runs low because it over-counts collisions.
-->
